findings.title: Tag 226a-2 schema-stale probe loops forever when FTS cache is post-v2-but-pre-Tag-211l
findings.description: FTS_CACHE_VERSION was set to 2 BEFORE Tag 211l added the annualSGA / annualDepreciation / annualShares fields. The Tag 211l comment block (line 1479-1483) acknowledges that old caches will return undefined for the new fields and defaults them to []. The Tag 226a-2 probe `_existingSnapshotMissingTag211lFields` (line 1240) detects snapshots that lack annualSGA/Depr/currentAssets and forces a full pull. But the full pull then re-uses the same stale FTS cache because `cached._cacheVersion !== FTS_CACHE_VERSION` is FALSE for v2 caches written between v2 introduction and Tag 211l. ftsAnnualSGA defaults to [] (line 1482), and the merge guard at line 1589 only sets `canonical.annual.annualSGA` when length > 0. So the rewritten snapshot is still missing annualSGA, the next pull's schema-stale probe fires again, full pull served from cache again, infinite loop.
findings.mechanism: Snapshot S has age<7d AND lacks annualSGA. processOne sees `staleSchema=true`, skips price-only, calls full-pull. Full-pull opens fundamentals-cache/<ticker>.json which has `_cacheVersion=2`, `cachedAt < 28d ago`, `payload.ftsAnnualSGA = undefined`. useCache=true, ftsAnnualSGA = (undefined) || [] = []. ftsAnnualSGA.length === 0 so the guard at line 1589 does NOT assign canonical.annual.annualSGA. Snapshot is rewritten without annualSGA → next day same path again. The probe forces yf.quoteSummary + yf.quote on every affected ticker every day, burning the rate-limit budget that the price-only fast-path was designed to save (~5-7 HTTP calls per ticker × every-affected-ticker × every-day, vs ~1 call price-only).
findings.reproducer: Pre-flight: pick any ticker whose `fundamentals-cache/<ticker>.json` was written between FTS_CACHE_VERSION=2 introduction and Tag 211l and is <28 days old AND whose snapshot lacks annualSGA. Run pull-yahoo with PULL_CONCURRENCY=1 and observe the log: same ticker prints `[schema-stale]: forcing full pull to backfill Tag 211l fields` on every consecutive run.
findings.impact: (1) Wasted rate-limit budget — every affected ticker (probe Tag 225d showed 0/100 sampled snapshots had Tag 211l fields, so ~98% of universe at one point) gets the expensive full-pull treatment on every run instead of the cheap price-only fast-path. Run #107 already produced 7,210 rate-limit failures; this defect compounds the load. (2) annualSGA / annualDepreciation / annualShares stay missing → beneish-m-score, ohlson-o-score, buyback-yield, sga-revenue-trend, working-capital-trend stay computable=false for those tickers indefinitely (until the 28-day cache expires naturally). (3) The schema-stale probe was supposed to solve exactly this problem; the comment at line 1236-1239 claims `the cache file's _cacheVersion is undefined for pre-Tag-211l caches` — that's wrong: v2 was introduced before Tag 211l, so post-v2-pre-211l caches DO have _cacheVersion=2.
findings.suggested_fix: Bump FTS_CACHE_VERSION to 3 alongside this fix so post-v2-pre-211l caches are invalidated on next pull. Document at line 1443: 'v3: added ftsAnnualSGA, ftsAnnualDepreciation, ftsAnnualShares.' Alternative if cache invalidation cost is unacceptable: also invalidate the cache when the schema-stale probe fires (e.g., set useCache=false when staleSchema=true), so the schema-stale path always re-fetches FTS even though the cache says it's fresh.

findings.title: _priceOnlyUpdate scales Yahoo quote() by financialCurrency FX rate but Yahoo returns trading-currency
findings.description: After Tag 204 / Tag 219c, `meta.reportingCurrencyOriginal` is set to the FINANCIAL currency (e.g. TWD for TSM, CNY for BABA, HKD for 9988.HK) and `meta.fxRateApplied` is the FX factor from that financial currency to USD (e.g. TWD→USD ≈ 0.031). The price-only path multiplies the live `yf.quote()` output by this fxRateApplied value (lines 1345, 1355). But `yf.quote()` returns `regularMarketPrice` and `marketCap` in the TRADING currency, which for ADRs is the LISTING currency (USD for TSM/BABA/NU), NOT the financial reporting currency. For TSM at $200 USD live: existing.price.regularMarketPrice = 200 × 0.031 = $6.20 (~32× too small). Same defect for marketCap. The full-pull path is unaffected because `_convertSnapshotToUSD` scales the entire snapshot from financial currency × rate; the price-only path uniquely re-scales values that Yahoo already returned in USD.
findings.mechanism: Tag 204 (line 660-685) intentionally splits trading vs reporting currency: `tradingCurrency = _tc` and `rcOriginal = _fc` (financial). For TSM: rcOriginal='TWD', tradingCurrency='USD'. `_convertSnapshotToUSD` sets `meta.fxRateApplied = factor` where factor = FX_TO_USD['TWD'] ≈ 0.031 (line 343). On the next price-only update: origCcy='TWD' (non-USD), fxAppliedRaw=0.031 (finite), so `needsFx = fxApplied !== 1 && reportingCurrencyOriginal !== 'USD'` = true. `q.regularMarketPrice` = 200 USD (Yahoo returns in trading currency for ADRs). Line 1345: existing.price.regularMarketPrice = 200 * 0.031 = 6.20. The snapshot now contains a price 32× too low. Same line 1355 for marketCap. The next price-only run sees its own corrupted output as 'existing'; since the math doesn't re-multiply (existing.price is overwritten each run with q × fxApplied), the corruption is bounded to one factor, but the user-visible price/mcap remain wrong indefinitely.
findings.reproducer: Pre-flight: pick TSM, BABA, 9988.HK, NU, or any ADR where `meta.tradingCurrency !== meta.reportingCurrencyOriginal`. Confirm the snapshot's `price.regularMarketPrice` matches the recent USD trading price. Run pull-yahoo; observe the new snapshot's regularMarketPrice is now 1/(FX rate) — e.g., 0.031 × original for TSM. Then check marketCap: should be ~$1.1T-ish for TSM but will show $34B-ish.
findings.impact: Every ADR ticker with `tradingCurrency !== reportingCurrencyOriginal` gets its price and mcap silently corrupted on every price-only refresh (~80% of pulls). MarketCap-floor filter at line 1361-1366 may then incorrectly delete the snapshot ('price-only floor: mcap=0.034B < $1B'). Watchlist effectively loses TSM, BABA, NU, 9988.HK, and every other major non-US-domiciled ADR. The mcap floor delete is irreversible without re-discovery. Universe shrinks silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Yahoo's quote() endpoint returns prices and marketCap in trading currency, not reporting currency. The price-only update should convert from TRADING currency to USD, not from financial currency to USD. (a) Store `meta.tradingCurrency` (already done at line 696) AND a `meta.tradingFxRateApplied` separately from the financial fxRateApplied. (b) In _priceOnlyUpdate, look up FX_TO_USD[existing.meta.tradingCurrency.toUpperCase()] and use THAT rate. For US-domiciled stocks tradingCurrency=USD → rate=1 → no-op. For TSM tradingCurrency=USD → rate=1, no scaling. For pure local-listing like 6502.T tradingCurrency='JPY' → rate ≈ 0.0067 → correct conversion. Note: pull-yahoo never persists FX_TO_USD lookup for trading currency today, so a small refactor of the price-only path or a lazy lookup against the in-memory FX_TO_USD is required.

findings.title: mapFTSToBalance row-skip rule misaligns annualBalance with annualRev
findings.description: mapFTSToBalance iterates fts.annualBs and only pushes a row when `cash != null || totalDebt != null || totalAssets != null` (line 1016). mapFTSToAnnual (called on fts.annualFin) skips rows where rev==null AND every derivative is null (line 967). The two FTS modules are independent fetches → different year coverage and different per-year completeness. Any year where annualFin returned rev but annualBs returned all-null (rare but possible — e.g. Yahoo's bs response truncated to 4 years while annualFin has 5) silently drops that year from annualBalance but keeps it in annualRev. canonical.annual.annualBalance[i] then references a DIFFERENT calendar year than annualRev[i]. Methods consuming the row directly — e.g. ROIC = annualOpInc[i] / (annualBalance[i].totalDebt + annualBalance[i].totalAssets) — silently mix years.
findings.mechanism: Suppose Yahoo returns 5 annualFin rows (2020-2024) all with revenue, and 4 annualBs rows (2021-2024). mapFTSToAnnual → annualRev.length=5 (latest-first: 2024,2023,2022,2021,2020). mapFTSToBalance → annualBalance.length=4 (latest-first: 2024,2023,2022,2021). canonical merge keeps both as-is. Methods doing `annualRev[i] / annualBalance[i].totalAssets` compute correct ratios for i=0..3 but at i=4 (2020) annualRev=$X but annualBalance is undefined OR — worse, if FTS sometimes truncates the OLDEST year in annualBs and the NEWEST in annualFin or vice versa — alignment is broken at every index. F-DP-030/031 (existing fix) caught the rev-side alignment but only across SBC/Capex/RnD inside _ftsExtractByYear; the balance series uses its own skip-rule.
findings.reproducer: Pick a recent IPO with 3-year history where Yahoo's FTS balance-sheet is missing year-0 (very common). Inspect canonical.annual.annualRev.length vs annualBalance.length — they will differ. ROIC and net-debt-to-EBITDA methods then silently compute year-mismatched ratios.
findings.impact: ROIC, net-debt-EBITDA, asset-turnover, working-capital-trend, ohlson-o-score, beneish-m-score all consume annualBalance[i] positionally. A 1-year mis-alignment shifts every ratio by one fiscal year, hiding deterioration or false-flagging it. Silent — no warning emitted.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the F-DP-030/031 fix pattern to mapFTSToBalance too: either (a) push a null/empty placeholder row when all balance fields are null but the year exists in annualRev's year coverage, or (b) build a year-indexed dict first (Map<year, balanceRow>) and emit aligned arrays, or (c) tag every row with a fiscal year and align in consumer methods via year-matching.

findings.title: _convertSnapshotToUSD does not scale targetMeanPrice / targetMedianPrice — comparison with USD mcap broken
findings.description: Tag 220c surfaced `targetMeanPrice` and `targetMedianPrice` into metrics from `yahoo.financialData`. These are per-share price targets in the TRADING currency (Yahoo's documented convention). CCY_DENOMINATED_METRICS (line 316) explicitly omits them. After _convertSnapshotToUSD the snapshot's price/mcap are USD-normalized (via financial currency × rate), but targetMeanPrice / targetMedianPrice remain in trading currency. Any downstream method that compares price targets to price.regularMarketPrice or marketCap.value silently divides USD by JPY (or similar). The comment at line 781-783 marks them as 'ratios + counts + analyst price targets, so no FX implication' — that is incorrect for analyst price targets which ARE currency-denominated per-share values.
findings.mechanism: For 6502.T (Toshiba, JPY) Yahoo financialData.targetMeanPrice ≈ ¥6000. After mapper, metrics.targetMeanPrice.value = 6000. _convertSnapshotToUSD does NOT scale it (not in CCY_DENOMINATED_METRICS). marketCap.value gets scaled by ~0.0067 (JPY→USD) → mcap in USD. A 'analyst-upside' method computing (targetMeanPrice − currentPrice) / currentPrice silently mixes a JPY price target with a JPY currentPrice (price.regularMarketPrice is trading-currency too, never scaled by _convertSnapshotToUSD) — actually OK for that one method because both sides are JPY. BUT comparing targetMeanPrice (JPY) to anything USD-denominated (like a multiple expansion target derived from USD mcap) yields a 150× error for JPY. Tag 211h's analyst-revision-breadth + future expected-return methods are at risk.
findings.reproducer: Inspect any non-USD-listed snapshot (e.g. 6502.T, 600837.SS, NESN.SW). After full pull: metrics.targetMeanPrice.value will match Yahoo's raw value in local currency (JPY/CNY/CHF), but marketCap.value is USD. The two are no longer in the same currency.
findings.impact: Currently low because no shipped method directly combines targetMeanPrice with marketCap. Becomes HIGH if any future method (analyst-upside-vs-USD-target, expected-return-relative-to-USD-cap) is added without realizing the FX inconsistency. Snapshots also leak inconsistent units, complicating ad-hoc dashboarding.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'targetMeanPrice' and 'targetMedianPrice' to CCY_DENOMINATED_METRICS — BUT scale them by the TRADING-currency rate, not the financial-currency rate. Since _convertSnapshotToUSD currently uses a single `factor` derived from reportingCurrency, this requires either a separate trading-currency factor or pulling price targets in a converted form. Cleanest: persist `meta.tradingCurrency` (already done line 696) plus a sibling `meta.tradingFxRateApplied`, then scale per-share fields by that. If not feasible immediately, document explicitly that targetMeanPrice is in trading currency and warn downstream consumers.

findings.title: marketCap from quoteSummary.summaryDetail may already be USD for ADRs — _convertSnapshotToUSD double-converts
findings.description: After Tag 204/Tag 219c, the mapper sets `reportingCurrency = financialCurrency` (e.g., TWD for TSM) and `_convertSnapshotToUSD` multiplies marketCap by the financial-currency FX rate. But Yahoo's `summaryDetail.marketCap` for many ADRs is already reported in the TRADING currency (USD for TSM/BABA/NU). Empirical evidence: legacy AAPL.json (pre-fix) inspected shows `reportingCurrency: 'NasdaqGS'` — a stale region/exchange string, not a currency code — indicating the legacy code path stored a non-currency value in this field and _convertSnapshotToUSD's USD early-return then preserved the marketCap as-is. For Tag-204-converted ADR snapshots: marketCap (USD from Yahoo) × TWD→USD rate (0.031) yields a 32× shrunken mcap → the MIN_MCAP=$1B filter at line 1647 then incorrectly deletes the snapshot.
findings.mechanism: TSM real mcap ≈ $1.1T USD. Yahoo's summaryDetail.marketCap returns ~1.1e12 (USD). Mapper writes marketCap.value=1.1e12, meta.reportingCurrency='TWD'. _convertSnapshotToUSD line 308: `snap.marketCap = scale(snap.marketCap)` with factor=0.031 → marketCap.value=3.4e10. Now MIN_MCAP=$1B passes (still above floor for TSM), but for medium ADRs (NU at ~$60B → ~$1.9B post-conversion — still passes) the floor edges in. For small ADRs around $30B real → $930M post-conversion, the floor DELETES the snapshot (line 1664 fs.unlinkSync). The deleted ADR is removed from the universe until next refresh-universe.js run. Compounds with F-DP-002 (price-only path also scales).
findings.reproducer: Find an ADR (BIDU, JD, BABA, NU, TSM) and inspect marketCap.value in the snapshot. If reportingCurrency=CNY/TWD/HKD and meta.fxConverted=true, marketCap will be ~30× too small. Cross-check against a financial site's mcap-USD figure.
findings.impact: Marketcap floor at $1B (line 1647) deletes any non-US ADR whose real USD mcap is below ~$32B (1B / 0.031 for TWD). For ADRs in the $1B–$30B real-USD range, snapshot is irrecoverably deleted on every pull — universe silently loses small/mid-cap ADRs. F-DP-002 simultaneously scales the price-only path the same wrong way, multiplying the damage.
findings.suggested_fix: Determine empirically which Yahoo fields are in financial vs trading currency for ADRs. If summaryDetail.marketCap is trading currency for ADRs but local-currency for native listings: add a guard in _convertSnapshotToUSD that detects `tradingCurrency !== reportingCurrencyOriginal` and uses the TRADING fx rate for marketCap (and the financial rate for annual.* series). Alternative: prefer Yahoo's price.marketCap (if available and known-trading-currency), or fetch USD mcap via a separate yf.quote() call and trust that as authoritative. Add a regression test that BABA/TSM/NU snapshot marketCap is in the $50B-$1T range after pull.

findings.title: sortByStaleness regex matches fetchedAt before asOf — price-only-refreshed snapshots look older than they are
findings.description: sortByStaleness uses regex `"(?:asOf|fetchedAt)"\s*:\s*"([^"]+)"` and takes the first match in the first 300 bytes. The mapper serialization order (line 686 onward) writes `fetchedAt` BEFORE `asOf`, so the regex matches `fetchedAt`. _priceOnlyUpdate updates `existing.meta.asOf = newAsOf` (line 1329) but never updates `existing.meta.fetchedAt`. Result: a snapshot refreshed today via price-only has asOf=today but fetchedAt=N-days-ago; sortByStaleness sees the N-days-ago timestamp and sorts the ticker EARLIER in the queue than its actual freshness. Combined with FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_DAYS=7, this only matters for ordering, not correctness — but it wastes the staleness-sort budget by re-queuing recently-touched tickers ahead of truly-stale ones.
findings.mechanism: Day 0: full pull writes fetchedAt=Day0, asOf=Day0. Days 1-6: price-only updates set asOf=DayN while fetchedAt stays Day0. Day 7: sortByStaleness reads fetchedAt=Day0 (7d old), ranks ticker among most-stale. _getExistingSnapshotAge (line 1213) reads asOf only and correctly sees Day6 → youngEnough=true → price-only path. But the ticker is queued early because of sort; it's still served fast → no real harm beyond ordering. The Tag 218 comment at line 1075-1078 says 'accept BOTH meta.asOf and meta.fetchedAt — pre-Tag-215j snapshots only had fetchedAt' — but the implementation greedily matches fetchedAt FIRST when both are present (because of serialization order), defeating the intent.
findings.reproducer: Inspect any snapshot that has been price-only-refreshed: meta.fetchedAt < meta.asOf. sortByStaleness sees fetchedAt; _getExistingSnapshotAge sees asOf. Confirm regex behavior with `'{"fetchedAt":"x","asOf":"y"}'.match(/"(?:asOf|fetchedAt)"\s*:\s*"([^"]+)"/)` → matches fetchedAt.
findings.impact: Sort-order inefficiency only. The downstream age check uses asOf, so freshness decisions are correct. But the original intent (Tag 218 audit fix to sort most-stale first) is partly defeated: tickers that have been price-only-refreshed daily for 5 days appear as 5-day-stale in the sort, ahead of tickers truly 6-days-stale. Wastes the first-pass priority slots on already-fresh tickers if the staleness-sort budget is meant to guarantee daily coverage of the truly-stale tail.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) update meta.fetchedAt alongside meta.asOf in _priceOnlyUpdate (semantically defensible: both reflect 'last successful pull, whatever kind'), or (b) change sortByStaleness regex to prefer asOf when both are present — match all occurrences and pick the latest timestamp via Math.max. Option (a) is one line.

findings.title: FTS cache write race during retry — concurrent workers can leave stale partial cache
findings.description: fundamentals-cache writes use writeFileAtomic (good — Tag 189 fix). But the cache READ (line 1448) is non-atomic and not serialized: a worker may read cached.payload after another worker has fsync'd the tmp file but BEFORE the rename completes. On Windows the rename retry loop (lib/atomic-write.js line 84-102) waits up to 380ms cumulatively for EBUSY/EPERM — during that window a concurrent reader sees the OLD cache file (with stale _cacheVersion or missing Tag 211l fields). The same ticker should not be processed by two workers simultaneously (workers pop unique idx), but a recently-failed ticker can re-enter via the retry path of `quoteSummaryWithRetry`. More importantly: a different ticker that shares a cache filename collision (via safeSnapshotFilename sanitization for special chars) could race — e.g., two distinct watchlist entries `TICK.A` and `TICK_A` both sanitize to `TICK_A.json`. Verified by reading safeSnapshotFilename: any non-A-Z0-9.- char becomes `_`, so ticker `TICK A` (with space) and `TICK_A` collide.
findings.mechanism: watchlist has two stocks with tickers that sanitize to the same filename (rare but possible after universe-merge from heterogeneous sources). Both processed concurrently. Worker A writes cache for tickerA, worker B reads cache for tickerB but gets tickerA's payload. Less rare scenario: a worker's full-pull retries (Tag 215f retries with 90s delay) re-enter processOne for the same ticker after partial state is written — but processOne is keyed by stock obj reference, not ticker → re-entry doesn't happen via the worker pool, only via internal yf.queue. So the race is mostly about ticker-name collision, not retry. Still real but narrow.
findings.reproducer: Add `TICK A` and `TICK_A` to watchlist (or any two tickers with `[^A-Z0-9.-]` chars that collapse to the same sanitized stem). Run pull-yahoo with PULL_CONCURRENCY≥2. Inspect fundamentals-cache: only one file exists for both, payloads overwrite each other.
findings.impact: Cache pollution: two different tickers share the same FTS cache → second ticker reuses first ticker's annualFin/annualCash. Snapshot's annual.* are silently wrong (different company's fundamentals). Probability low in practice (universe has few special-char tickers), but the failure mode is silent and severe when triggered.
findings.suggested_fix: Disambiguate the cache filename by including a stable hash of the original (unsanitized) ticker, e.g. `safeSnapshotFilename(ticker) + '.' + crc32(ticker)`. Or use the watchlist's `yahoo_symbol` (which is already disambiguated) as the cache key. As a separate fix, add a sanity check in the cache reader: if `cached.symbol !== stock.yahoo_symbol`, treat as miss and re-fetch.

findings.title: errClass 'mapper-bug' classification triggers no special handling — programming errors fail silently
findings.description: F-DP-006 (original) added a mapper-bug class for TypeError/ReferenceError to surface programming errors separately from transient Yahoo failures. The classification works (line 1734), and `console.error('MAPPER-BUG', e.stack)` prints the stack. But the failure is still pushed to `failures[]` with the same shape as transient errors and counts against the 75% fail-ratio threshold (line 1875 `failRatio > 0.75 ? 1 : 0`). A mapper bug affecting 80% of tickers crosses the threshold → process.exit(1) → CI fails LOUDLY. But a mapper bug affecting 5-50% of tickers (which is the more realistic case for a schema-drift bug in one specific code path) emits a wave of `MAPPER-BUG` console errors that the CI log filter may or may not surface. The 'not-found' branch (line 1759) marks meta.delisted=true, which is wrong for mapper-bug errors and could permanently delist a real ticker if the regex at line 1738 ever matches a mapper-bug stack trace.
findings.mechanism: Suppose Yahoo schema drifts so `summaryDetail.marketCap` becomes a string instead of number. mapYahooToCanonical's downstream code path attempts numeric ops → TypeError. The catch classifies as 'mapper-bug', stack is printed. The error message also contains the substring 'TypeError' which does NOT match the 'not-found' regex, so meta.delisted is NOT set. So this part is OK. But: if a mapper bug throws a custom error that happens to contain the phrase 'no data found' in its message (e.g. 'no data found in summaryDetail.marketCap'), then BOTH branches match — errClass='mapper-bug' set first via constructor name (TypeError takes precedence in the if/else if chain), so 'not-found' branch is NOT entered. OK in current code. The issue is the LOUDNESS gap: a 5-30% mapper-bug failure rate creates no separate alert, just buried `MAPPER-BUG` console.error lines.
findings.reproducer: Inject a mapper bug (e.g., `_y(undefined, 'foo', 'bar')`) into one branch of the mapper. Run on a watchlist of 1000 tickers. Observe: ~1000 'MAPPER-BUG' lines in the log, failures.length=1000, n_failed=1000, fail-ratio 100%, process.exit(1). For 30% bug: 300 failures, fail-ratio 30%, exit 0 → CI passes, downstream methods see 30% missing snapshots. No dedicated alert.
findings.impact: Mapper bugs in low-frequency code paths (FX failure branches, sector-financial fallback, ADR detection) silently corrupt 5-20% of snapshots without firing the fail-ratio gate. Downstream methods see missing data but assume Yahoo-side issue, not code bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a separate exit-1 gate for mapper-bug rate: `const mapperBugs = failures.filter(f => f.errClass === 'mapper-bug').length; if (mapperBugs > 0) { _log('ERROR', `${mapperBugs} mapper bugs — likely code regression`); process.exit(2); }`. Any mapper-bug is by definition a code defect; CI should fail immediately. Also emit a GitHub-Actions error annotation (`::error::`) so the failure surfaces in the workflow summary.

findings.title: refresh-universe.js fetchScreener silently returns [] on ANY exception — silent shrink-by-screener
findings.description: fetchScreener wraps `yf.screener(...)` in a try/catch that returns `[]` on any exception with no logging. The companion fetchExchangePage (line 144-174) was hardened in F-DP-037 / Tag 190 to return `{quotes, error}` and bubble errors up for per-exchange stats. fetchScreener did not get the same treatment. A 429 rate-limit hit, a Cloudflare block, or a Yahoo schema-validation error on the predefined-screener endpoint produces `[]` from fetchScreener, which the caller treats as 'no candidates in this region/bucket' (line 200: `if (quotes.length === 0) continue;`). Run #107 referenced ~50MB of suppressed schema-validation logs — exactly the kind of failure that would surface as `[]` from this code path. Combined with 25 regions × 13 screener IDs = 325 chances for silent failure, the universe can shrink invisibly. The audit context note 'auto-universe refresh might miss IPOs' is exactly this failure mode.
findings.mechanism: yf.screener({ scrIds: 'most_actives', region: 'US' }) throws (any reason: 429, schema validation, network). Line 131 catches, returns []. Caller continues to next bucket without recording the failure. Per-region/per-bucket stats are not collected. If 8 of 25 regions blanket-fail (Cloudflare geo-block on EM proxies), the universe quietly loses ~30% of regional candidates without any signal. exchangeStats[] (line 286) covers the custom-screener path but not the predefined-bucket path.
findings.reproducer: Mock yf.screener to throw 'HTTP 429' for region='US' regardless of scrIds. Run refresh-universe. Observe: no error logs from the bucket loop; only the per-region '---' header lines and zero 'id: N -> M' lines for US. universe candidates is silently smaller without any alarm.
findings.impact: New IPOs and recently-uplisted tickers in failing regions never enter the watchlist. Universe coverage degrades silently. The 'discovery source error' log (line 321) covers the additional sources but not yf.screener. Particularly bad because the multi-region screener loop is the PRIMARY discovery path; the other sources (SEC, NASDAQ-API, Finnhub) only cover US.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the F-DP-037 / Tag 190 treatment to fetchScreener: return `{quotes, error}`, log non-empty error on the calling side, and accumulate per-region/per-bucket failure counts into a screenerStats map. Emit a warning at end-of-run if any bucket has 100% failure rate or if total failures > 10%. Optionally retry on 429 with exponential backoff (same DELAYS pattern as quoteSummaryWithRetry).

findings.title: FX fallback rate of 1.0 missing for USD-key absence path; _convertToUSD returns raw value silently
findings.description: _convertToUSD (line 203) returns `value` unchanged if `FX_TO_USD[currency.toUpperCase()]` is null/undefined. Same pattern in _convertSnapshotToUSD line 266-274: if rate==null sets fxConversionFailed=true and returns. Defensive fallback is OK in isolation — the snapshot gets flagged. BUT in refresh-fx.js a corrupt fx-rates.json triggers the recovery branch (line 75-94) that re-runs from scratch — meanwhile pull-yahoo's loadFx (line 132) silently fails when the FX file is being written, returning early with FX_TO_USD=FX_FALLBACK and FX_SOURCE='fallback-hardcoded'. Pre-Tag-181 this meant entire pulls used 2024-stale hardcoded rates without alarm. Tag 181 added the warning at line 281, but the warning only fires PER STOCK during conversion, not once at fx-load time. Combined with FX_FALLBACK missing several currencies that refresh-fx fetches (TWD, BRL fallback is hardcoded at 0.031/0.20 — values from Q1 2024) means ANY currency whose live rate diverged 20%+ since 2024 (TRY definitely, ZAR, BRL maybe) is silently mispriced when fx-rates.json fails to load.
findings.mechanism: fx-rates.json read fails at line 132 (rare but possible: file-locked during refresh-fx tmp-rename window, JSON parse error from partial write, file not yet written on first-run). loadFx() catches at line 199, logs `[FX] fx-rates.json load failed:` (one log line only), and returns. FX_TO_USD remains FX_FALLBACK (2024-stale hardcoded). FX_SOURCE='fallback-hardcoded'. The per-snapshot warning at line 281 fires for every non-USD ticker (~5000+ messages). But the operator sees only 'FX-FALLBACK' warnings scattered through a 19k-ticker log — easy to overlook. fxRateApplied is set to the fallback factor (e.g. TRY=0.029 from 2024, vs live ≈ 0.026 in 2026 — 11% error for TRY). meta.fxRateSource='hardcoded-fallback' so per-snapshot it's documented, but no run-summary statistic flags 'X% of snapshots used fallback FX' for CI gates.
findings.reproducer: Delete or corrupt fx-rates.json (e.g. write `not json`). Run pull-yahoo on a watchlist with non-USD tickers. Observe: one '[FX] fx-rates.json load failed' line, then a 'FX-FALLBACK' warning per stock. Inspect the snapshots: meta.fxRateSource='hardcoded-fallback'. No CI gate fails because n_ok is computed independently of fxRateSource.
findings.impact: Silent acceptance of 2024-stale FX rates on every non-USD ticker for the duration of any fx-rates.json outage. Multi-percent error accumulates across the universe — affects all USD-denominated ratios (mcap, EV, revenueTTM, fcf-yield). Cross-ticker comparisons (regional rotation, sector-relative valuation) become unreliable. The error stays present until refresh-fx.js next succeeds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard CI gate: at end of pullAll, count snapshots with `meta.fxRateSource === 'hardcoded-fallback'`. If count exceeds 5% of non-USD universe AND fx-rates.json is older than FX_STALE_DAYS (already captured), emit ::error:: and process.exit(1). Alternatively: refuse to start pull-yahoo at all if fx-rates.json is missing/corrupt AND any non-USD ticker is in the watchlist (the engine treats stale FX as a structural data-quality failure rather than per-ticker degradation).

findings.title: pull-historical-prices.js uses simple batched Promise.all — slowest ticker blocks each batch
findings.description: While pull-yahoo migrated to a worker-pool pattern (Tag 163 — runWorkerPool) where a stalled ticker only blocks its own slot, pull-historical-prices.js still uses batched Promise.all (line 138-139). A single hung Yahoo socket inside a CONCURRENCY=10 batch holds up all 10 slots until the chart-call completes or times out. There is no per-call timeout on `yf.chart()` (unlike pull-yahoo which uses _withTimeout). For a 15,734-ticker watchlist with CONCURRENCY=10, this means 1574 batches; a 60s socket stall on any one batch adds 60s × N stalls to the total run time. Run #107's 165-min CI timeout context becomes relevant — pull-historical may fail to finish within budget under adverse network conditions.
findings.mechanism: yf.chart(stock.yahoo_symbol, ...) hangs on a socket timeout. No _withTimeout wrapper. Promise.all in line 139 waits for ALL 10 batch members. The 9 other tickers in the batch complete, but the batch promise doesn't resolve until the hung call eventually times out (default Node http timeout depends on agent config — can be minutes). Over 1500+ batches this is a real budget killer.
findings.reproducer: Run pull-historical-prices on a watchlist where one ticker reliably stalls Yahoo's chart endpoint (some delisted intl tickers do). Observe a 60-300s pause every time that ticker's batch runs.
findings.impact: Wall-clock inefficiency. May contribute to the CI 165-min step timeout occasionally hitting pull-historical-prices.
findings.suggested_fix: Migrate to the same worker-pool pattern pull-yahoo uses, with a _withTimeout wrapper around yf.chart (suggest 15s — chart() returns 400 daily quotes and shouldn't take longer than that). Also add a try/catch + timeout around the dedicated SPY pull at line 155.

findings.title: discovery/finnhub.js redirect handler uses unbounded recursion and may follow non-absolute Location
findings.description: discovery/finnhub.js's get() helper (line 37-38) handles 301/302 redirects with `return get(res.headers.location)` — no max-redirect cap, no relative-URL resolution, no res.resume() to drain the 3xx body. discovery/sec-tickers.js was hardened in Tag 229c-2 (line 18-76) to fix exactly these three issues. discovery/wikipedia-indices.js (line 27-28) and discovery/nasdaq-all.js (line 35-36) and discovery/otc-markets.js (line 40-41) and discovery/nasdaq-api.js (line 52-53) all share the same defect. None of these were fixed alongside sec-tickers despite affecting the same trust boundary.
findings.mechanism: If the upstream (Finnhub / Wikipedia / NASDAQ / OTC Markets) ever responds with a relative Location header — e.g. `Location: /api/v1/foo` — the URL constructor throws ERR_INVALID_URL inside https.get because relative paths aren't accepted. The catch in fetchFinnhubUniverse turns into a 'failed' log line; the entire exchange's contribution drops. With 17 exchanges and a 1100ms per-exchange delay, a single misconfigured CDN can wipe 100% of Finnhub-sourced discoveries. The unbounded recursion means a redirect loop crashes the process (stack overflow). The unleaked socket means under a multi-hop redirect chain the agent pool can leak.
findings.reproducer: Mock https.get to return 302 with `Location: /relative-path`. Call fetchFinnhubUniverse for any exchange. Observe ERR_INVALID_URL → empty Map. Repeat with a redirect loop (A → B → A) → call stack exceeded.
findings.impact: Finnhub, Wikipedia, NASDAQ-Trader, OTC-Markets, NASDAQ-API all share the same fragile pattern. Any future redirect setup (which is normal — these are external APIs maintained by 3rd parties) can silently drop a discovery source. Sec-tickers was fixed; the others were not.
findings.suggested_fix: Lift the hardened get() helper from sec-tickers.js (lines 38-76) into a shared lib/http-fetch.js module and import it from all five discovery files. Identical surface, removes drift across 5 files of duplicate code.
